items.html:
Complaints are a legal duty, not a favour

Handling complaints well is something you should want to do — but it is also something the law requires. Every adult social care service in England must, by law, run a proper complaints procedure. You cannot choose to ignore a complaint, brush it aside, or treat the person raising it as a nuisance.

The main rules are set by the Local Authority Social Services and NHS Complaints (England) Regulations 2009. These regulations cover complaints about both adult social care and NHS services, and they set out what a lawful procedure must do: make it easy for people to complain, name a responsible person who oversees the process, acknowledge each complaint promptly, investigate it properly, and give a clear response.

Complaints are a regulated activity

On top of those regulations, the Care Quality Commission (CQC) inspects how services handle complaints. CQC Regulation 16, “Receiving and acting on complaints”, says every provider must operate an accessible system to receive, handle and respond to complaints, investigate them, and take any action the investigation shows is needed.

  • It is inspected: the CQC can ask to see your complaints records as evidence of a safe, well-led service.
  • It is everyone’s business: the legal duty sits with the provider, but it is met through the everyday actions of workers like you.
  • It protects people: a strong procedure is one of the ways poor care is caught and put right.

items.question: Which of these best describes the legal position on complaints in adult social care in England?

answers.text: Services must run a proper complaints procedure under the 2009 Complaints Regulations, and the CQC inspects this under Regulation 16.
answers.feedback: Correct. The Complaints Regulations 2009 require a lawful procedure, and CQC Regulation 16 makes handling complaints a regulated, inspected activity.

answers.text: Handling complaints is good practice but there is no law that requires it.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. It is a legal duty under the 2009 regulations and is regulated by the CQC under Regulation 16.

answers.text: Only the NHS has to follow complaints law; adult social care services are exempt.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The 2009 regulations cover adult social care as well as the NHS, and the CQC regulates social care providers.

answers.text: A complaint only has to be dealt with if the person puts it in writing and signs it.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The law does not require a signed written complaint; services must accept and act on concerns raised in many ways.

items.html:
Staying calm when someone is upset

Often a complaint reaches you first, in the moment, from someone who is frustrated, frightened or angry. How you respond in those first few seconds shapes everything that follows. The most important thing is to stay calm and not take it personally — even when the words feel sharp, the person is usually reacting to a situation, not attacking you.

Resist the urge to defend yourself, argue back, or explain why they are wrong. Getting defensive tells the person you are more interested in protecting yourself than in helping them. Instead, listen. Give the person your full attention, let them finish without interrupting, and show with your face and body that you are taking them seriously.

A simple structure to remember

When your mind goes blank, it helps to have a few steps to fall back on. A widely used approach can be remembered as LEARN:

  • Listen: hear the person out fully before you respond.
  • Empathise: acknowledge how they feel — “I can see this has really upset you.”
  • Apologise: say sorry that they have had this experience, even before anything is investigated.
  • React: explain what you will do next and who you will tell.
  • Notify: make sure the concern reaches the right person so it is not lost.

Empathy and active listening cost nothing, yet they defuse most situations and often turn a tense moment into the start of trust being rebuilt.

items.question: When someone complains to you angrily in the moment, it is best to explain straight away why they have misunderstood the situation, so they calm down.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Jumping in to explain why they are wrong comes across as defensive; listening, empathising and apologising for their experience works far better.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Leaping in to correct the person sounds defensive and makes things worse. Listen first, empathise, and say sorry for their experience before anything else.

items.html:
Removing the fear of complaining

The people you support are often in a vulnerable position, and many worry that if they complain their care will suffer, or that staff will treat them differently. This fear of reprisal is one of the biggest reasons complaints go unspoken. Part of your job is to make it clear, in words and in how you behave, that raising a concern is welcome and will never be held against them.

Complaining should also be genuinely possible for everyone, not just those who are confident and articulate. That means offering information in accessible formats, giving people time, and helping them put their concerns into words if they find that hard.

Advocacy and communication needs

Some people will need extra support to be heard, and you should know where to turn.

  • Independent advocacy: anyone can ask for an independent advocate, and NHS Complaints Advocacy is free and available to help people make a complaint about health or social care.
  • Communication needs: use easy-read leaflets, pictures, interpreters, or communication aids so the person’s own voice comes through.
  • People who know them: with the person’s agreement, family or friends can help express a concern that is hard to put into words.

Never assume someone has no complaint simply because they cannot easily tell you one.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about supporting people to complain.
items.textField: No one should fear *reprisal* for raising a concern about their care. Where a person struggles to put a complaint into words, an independent *advocate* can speak up on their behalf. Information should be offered in accessible formats such as *easy-read* so everyone can understand it. For someone who does not speak English, an *interpreter* helps them to be heard. Never assume a person who cannot easily speak has no *concern* worth listening to.

items.html:
Record it and get it investigated

A complaint that stays in your head helps no one. Write it down accurately and factually as soon as you can: what was said, who by, when, and what you did in response. Stick to what actually happened rather than your opinion of it, and pass the record to the right person so the complaint can be looked into properly.

Timescales matter. Under the Complaints Regulations, a complaint should be acknowledged promptly — usually within three working days — and then investigated and responded to within the timescale agreed with the person. Keeping people informed while an investigation is underway is as important as the final answer.

Being open and apologising well

When something has gone wrong, people deserve honesty. The statutory duty of candour (CQC Regulation 20) requires providers to be open and transparent, to tell people when they have been harmed, and to say sorry. Being open is not weakness — it is the law and it is what rebuilds trust.

  • Apologise sincerely: saying sorry is not an admission of legal liability, so never withhold an apology out of fear.
  • Explain honestly: tell people what happened and what you have found, in plain language.
  • Say what will change: a good response sets out the action being taken so it does not happen again.

items.question: A relative complains that their mother missed her lunchtime medication. What is the best response?

answers.text: Record the facts, acknowledge the complaint promptly, apologise sincerely, and pass it on to be investigated with the relative kept informed.
answers.feedback: Correct. Accurate recording, a prompt acknowledgement, an honest apology and a proper investigation reflect both the regulations and the duty of candour.

answers.text: Avoid apologising in case saying sorry counts as admitting the service is legally at fault.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. An apology is not an admission of legal liability, and the duty of candour expects openness and a sincere sorry.

answers.text: Reassure the relative it was probably nothing and there is no need to record it formally.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Playing a concern down and failing to record it breaches both good practice and the complaints procedure.

answers.text: Wait to see whether they complain again before doing anything about it.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Complaints must be acknowledged and acted on promptly, not left to see if the person persists.

items.html:
Look for themes and trends

A single complaint tells you about one moment. Many complaints, looked at together, tell you about your service. This is where the real value of complaints lies: not just in fixing one problem, but in spotting the patterns behind them.

When complaints are logged and reviewed over time, themes appear — perhaps calls for help take too long at night, or a particular meal is regularly cold. On their own each grumble seems small; grouped together they point clearly to something that needs to change. Reviewing trends turns scattered complaints into useful information the whole team can act on.

Close the loop and share the learning

Improvement only counts if people can see it. A simple “you said, we did” approach — telling people what you changed as a result of their feedback — shows complaints are taken seriously and encourages others to speak up.

A realistic scenario. Over a month, three families mention that evening drinks are served too early and residents get thirsty overnight. Individually the staff had reassured each family and moved on. At the team meeting the pattern is noticed, the routine is changed so a later drinks round is added, and a note on the noticeboard tells families what was done. A handful of small complaints becomes a real improvement in care.

items.question: In the scenario, what turned three separate comments about evening drinks into a genuine improvement?

answers.text: The comments were logged and reviewed together, the pattern was spotted at a team meeting, the routine was changed, and families were told.
answers.feedback: Correct. Reviewing complaints for themes, acting on the pattern and feeding back “you said, we did” is how complaints drive improvement.

answers.text: Each family was reassured on the day, so nothing further was needed.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Reassuring people one by one is exactly what stopped the pattern being seen and the problem being fixed.

answers.text: The complaints were kept separate so no one would think there was a wider issue.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Keeping them separate hides the trend; it is bringing them together that reveals the problem.

answers.text: The families were asked to stop raising the same point repeatedly.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Discouraging feedback is the opposite of learning from complaints and would let the problem continue.
